section.title: David Spares Saul Again
section.sectionRationale: For a second time the Ziphites betray David's location, and for a second time Saul comes hunting him with three thousand men, and for a second time David finds Saul utterly defenseless, this time asleep in the middle of his own camp. David slips in, takes the spear and water jug from beside Saul's head as proof, and again refuses to let Abishai strike the LORD's anointed. It is one movement: the settled conviction, tested twice now, that David must not seize the throne by violence but leave Saul's fate entirely to the LORD.
section.thesis: Given a second total opportunity to end his exile by killing Saul, David again refuses to lift his hand against the LORD's anointed, entrusting the timing and manner of Saul's downfall to God alone, a fixed resolve that marks him as a type of the greater King who would not grasp His kingdom by force.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Saul renews the hunt, verses 1 to 5; David and Abishai in the sleeping camp, verses 6 to 12; David's taunt across the ravine to Abner, verses 13 to 16; the exchange between David and Saul, verses 17 to 20; and Saul's confession and blessing, verses 21 to 25.

verses.text: Then the Ziphites came to Saul at Gibeah and said, “Is not David hiding on the hill of Hachilah, opposite Jeshimon?”
So Saul, accompanied by three thousand chosen men of Israel, went down to the Wilderness of Ziph to search for David there.
Saul camped beside the road at the hill of Hachilah opposite Jeshimon, but David was living in the wilderness. When he realized that Saul had followed him there,
David sent out spies to verify that Saul had arrived.

Then David set out and went to the place where Saul had camped. He saw the place where Saul and Abner son of Ner, the general of his army, had lain down. Saul was lying inside the inner circle of the camp, with the troops camped around him.
David asked Ahimelech the Hittite and Abishai son of Zeruiah, Joab’s brother, “Who will go down with me to Saul in the camp?” “I will go with you,” answered Abishai.

That night David and Abishai came to the troops, and Saul was lying there asleep in the inner circle of the camp, with his spear stuck in the ground by his head. And Abner and the troops were lying around him.

Abishai said to David, “Today God has delivered your enemy into your hand. Now, therefore, please let me thrust the spear through him into the ground with one stroke. I will not need to strike him twice!”
But David said to Abishai, “Do not destroy him, for who can extend a {{yad|hand}} against the LORD’s {{mashiach|anointed}} and be guiltless?”
David added, “As surely as the LORD lives, the LORD Himself will strike him down; either his day will come and he will die, or he will go into battle and perish.
But the LORD forbid that I should extend my hand against the LORD’s anointed. Instead, take the spear and water jug by his head, and let us go.”

So David took the spear and water jug by Saul’s head, and they departed. No one saw them or knew about it, nor did anyone wake up; they all remained asleep, because a deep sleep from the LORD had fallen on them.
Then David crossed to the other side and stood atop the mountain at a distance; there was a wide gulf between them.
And David shouted to the troops and to Abner son of Ner, “Will you not answer me, Abner?” “Who are you who calls to the king?” Abner replied.
So David said to Abner, “You are a man, aren’t you? And who in Israel is your equal? Why then did you not protect your lord the king when one of the people came to destroy him?

This thing you have done is not good. As surely as the LORD lives, all of you deserve to die, since you did not protect your lord, the LORD’s anointed. Now look around. Where are the king’s spear and water jug that were by his head?”

units.label: David appeals to Saul
units.note: what evil is in my hand

verses.text: Then Saul recognized David’s voice and asked, “Is that your voice, David my son?” “It is my voice, my lord and king,” David said.
And he continued, “Why is my lord pursuing his servant? What have I done? What evil is in my hand?

Now please, may my lord the king hear the words of his servant: If the LORD has stirred you up against me, then may He accept an offering. But if men have done it, may they be cursed in the presence of the LORD! For today they have driven me away from sharing in the inheritance of the LORD, saying, ‘Go, serve other gods.’
So do not let my blood fall to the ground far from the presence of the LORD. For the king of Israel has come out to look for a flea, like one who hunts a partridge in the mountains.”
Then Saul replied, “I have sinned. Come back, David my son. I will never harm you again, because today you considered my life precious. I have played the fool and have committed a grave error!”
“Here is the king’s spear,” David answered. “Let one of the young men come over and get it.

May the LORD repay every man for his {{tsedaqah|righteousness}} and faithfulness. For the LORD delivered you into my hand today, but I would not extend my hand against the LORD’s {{mashiach|anointed}}.
As surely as I valued your life today, so may the LORD value my life and rescue me from all trouble.”
Saul said to him, “May you be blessed, David my son. You will accomplish great things and will surely prevail.” So David went on his way, and Saul returned home.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one. Saul was set apart for the throne by Samuel's anointing oil, and even though he hunts David without cause, David twice refuses to lift a hand against him for this reason alone, verses 9 and 23. The office belongs to the LORD's own choosing, not to whoever can seize it by the sword.

tsedaqah.gloss: Righteousness, right conduct, covenant faithfulness. David asks that the LORD Himself repay every man his tsedaqah, resting his reward on God's just character rather than on Saul's unstable gratitude, since Saul's own promises have already failed once before, in chapter 24.

yad.gloss: Hand, the seat of a person's power or action. Twice David refuses to extend his yad against the LORD's anointed, and he later asks Saul what evil is in his own hand, verse 18, framing the whole episode as a question of whose hand will act, and whose hand will be found innocent.

basic.context: This is the second time in as many episodes that David has Saul completely in his power. The Ziphites betray his location again, Saul comes hunting him with three thousand men, and David slips by night into the very heart of the sleeping camp, right beside Saul's head. His companion Abishai begs to end it with one thrust of the spear. But David refuses, a second time, to kill the man God anointed as king. Instead he takes the spear and water jug as evidence and shouts the proof across the valley at dawn, rebuking Abner for failing to guard the king. Confronted, Saul again confesses his sin and blesses David, though the two do not travel home together; David still will not trust his safety to Saul's word. The whole scene asks how an anointed but rejected king is meant to wait for God's kingdom rather than seize it for himself.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing David's own conviction about a specific king whom God had anointed; it is not a general command that every wrong must go unanswered or that lawful justice may never be pursued. What it does teach every reader is a settled trust that vindication and timing belong to the LORD, especially when we are wronged by those set in authority over us.
basic.originalLanguages: David will not raise a yad, hand, against the LORD's mashiach, anointed, even though Saul hunts him without cause. At the end he asks the LORD to repay every man his tsedaqah, righteousness, the very thing Saul's own word cannot reliably deliver.
basic.typology: David, God's anointed yet still uncrowned king, refuses again and again to seize the throne by violence, choosing instead to wait for God to act in His own time and way. This looks forward to great David's greater Son, who though truly King would not call down legions of angels or grasp an earthly throne by force, entrusting Himself instead to the Father's justice and timing.
basic.christ: David's refusal to strike the LORD's anointed, even when a companion urges it and the opportunity is total, anticipates the pattern fulfilled perfectly in Jesus Christ. Jesus, the true Anointed One, refused Peter's sword in Gethsemane and would not summon armies of angels to seize His kingdom by force, choosing instead the Father's path through suffering to glory. Like David, He entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly, and received His kingdom not by grasping but by humble obedience even to death, only then exalted by God Himself.
basic.god: God is shown as the true King over kings, the one who alone has the right to remove Saul in His own timing, whether by natural death or in battle. He casts a supernatural deep sleep over Saul's whole camp so David can act without bloodshed, and it is to Him that David appeals for vindication and reward rather than to human strength or opportunity. Even Saul, in a fleeting moment of honesty, recognizes that David's restraint flows from valuing what belongs to the LORD.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 24 records the first time David spares Saul, in the cave at En-gedi. 1 Peter 2 says Christ entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly. Matthew 26 shows Jesus refusing the sword in Gethsemane. Romans 12 says vengeance belongs to the LORD. Philippians 2 says Christ did not grasp at equality with God but humbled Himself.
basic.oneStory: David's second refusal to kill Saul is one more step in the long story of a king who will not seize his throne by violence, trusting the LORD's timing instead. It anticipates the true Anointed One, Jesus, who also refused to grasp His kingdom by force, and whose patient trust in the Father's justice becomes the pattern for all who suffer unjustly while awaiting God's vindication.

context.literary: A deliberate echo of chapter 24, told with fresh details: the betrayal by the Ziphites, the night incursion into the sleeping camp, the taken spear and water jug as proof, the shouted rebuke across the valley, and Saul's confession. The repetition itself makes the point, David's restraint at En-gedi was not a one-time impulse but a settled, repeatable conviction.
context.historical: The wilderness of Ziph lies south of Hebron, rough hill country David used as one of several hideouts from Saul. A royal military camp of the period would post Saul, the commanding general Abner, and troops in a defensive ring; for two men to walk unchallenged into its center and out again was extraordinary, and the narrator credits it to a deep sleep from the LORD rather than mere carelessness.
context.canonical: This is the second and final test of the same principle established in chapter 24: God's anointed servant will not seize kingship by violence against another of God's anointed, however unjustly opposed. The pattern shapes how Israel, and later the New Testament, thinks about relating to flawed or hostile human authority while still awaiting God's own timing and justice.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show, for a second time, that David refused to kill Saul when he had complete power to do so, entrusting Saul's fate and his own vindication entirely to the LORD, so establishing beyond doubt that David's throne would come as a gift from God rather than a prize seized by the sword.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes David's specific choice toward a specific king anointed by God; it does not prescribe pacifism toward all enemies or forbid lawful justice in every circumstance. What endures is the principle of leaving vengeance and timing to God rather than grasping for what He has promised by our own violent hand.

debates.question: Why did David take the spear and water jug instead of simply leaving quietly?

views.label: Public proof of restraint
views.case: Taking these items let David prove beyond doubt, at a safe distance the next morning, that he had been near enough to kill Saul but chose not to, silencing any future accusation of disloyalty or rebellion.

views.label: Evidence that also indicted the guard
views.case: The missing royal spear and water jug proved not only David's restraint but Abner's failure to protect the king, turning the evidence into both a defense of David and a public rebuke of Saul's men, verses 15-16.

debates.bearing: Either way, David sought public vindication through visible proof rather than through violence, showing that being right before God does not require winning the argument by force.

debates.question: Does Saul's confession in verse 21 show genuine repentance?

views.label: A real if unstable softening
views.case: Saul's words echo his sorrow in chapter 24, and he goes further here, blessing David and predicting his future greatness, suggesting a moment of true, if fragile, conviction.

views.label: A repeated but hollow pattern
views.case: Saul had said nearly the same words once before, chapter 24, and would resume hunting David soon after; the repetition suggests emotional remorse without lasting change, since he offers David no restored place at court or lasting safety.

debates.bearing: David does not stake his safety on Saul's confession either way; he returns to his own men rather than to Saul's court, a wisdom that discerns between a felt emotion of sorrow and a proven change of behavior.

study.originalLanguages: The vocabulary keeps the whole scene tethered to God's authority rather than David's opportunity. Abishai sees Saul's helplessness as God's gift and reaches for his own yad, hand, to end it, but David insists no one may extend a yad against the LORD's mashiach, anointed, and be innocent. He leaves the manner and timing of Saul's end entirely to the LORD, either a natural day of dying or death in battle. Confronting Saul, he asks what evil is in his own yad, turning the moral question back on himself rather than accusing Saul outright. And in the end he asks the LORD to repay every man his tsedaqah, righteousness, resting his reward not on Saul's fickle gratitude but on God's own just character.

typology.type: David refusing a second total opportunity to seize the throne by violence
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Anointed One, who would not grasp an earthly kingdom by force but received all authority from the Father through suffering and resurrection, Philippians 2:6-11.

typology.type: David entrusting his cause to the LORD rather than avenging himself
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who when reviled did not revile in return but entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23.

study.christ: David's second refusal to kill Saul, even with a companion urging it and every advantage in his favor, anticipates the pattern fulfilled perfectly in Jesus Christ. Jesus, the true Anointed One, refused Peter's sword in Gethsemane, John 18:11, and would not call on twelve legions of angels to seize His kingdom by force, Matthew 26:53, choosing instead the Father's path through suffering to glory. Like David, He entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23, and received His kingdom not by grasping equality with God but through humble obedience even to death on a cross, only then exalted by God Himself to the highest place, Philippians 2:6-11.
study.god: God is the true King over kings, the one who alone has the right to remove Saul in His own timing, whether by natural death or in battle, verse 10. He supernaturally casts a deep sleep over Saul's entire camp so that David can act without bloodshed or risk, and it is to Him, not to Saul's fickle promises, that David appeals for reward and vindication, verse 23. Even Saul, in a fleeting moment of honesty, is made to confess that David's restraint flows from valuing what belongs to the LORD alone.

crossReferences.note: The first time David spares Saul, cutting only the corner of his robe in the cave at En-gedi, establishing the same principle this chapter repeats and confirms.
Jesus refuses Peter's sword and the offer of angelic armies, choosing the Father's path rather than seizing deliverance by force.
Christ entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly rather than retaliating when reviled and suffering unjustly.
Never take revenge, but leave room for God's wrath, the very posture David models toward Saul.
Christ did not grasp at equality with God but humbled Himself, and was afterward exalted by the Father, the pattern David's restraint foreshadows.

study.oneStory: David's second refusal to kill Saul is one more settled step in the long story of a king who will not seize his throne by violence, choosing instead to trust the LORD's own timing and justice. It anticipates the true Anointed One, Jesus, who also refused to grasp His kingdom by force, and whose patient trust in the Father becomes the pattern for every believer who must wait on God's vindication rather than seize it for themselves.

quiz.q: When David found Saul asleep in the camp a second time, what did he do?

options.text: He refused to let Abishai kill Saul, insisting no one may harm the LORD's anointed, and instead took the spear and water jug as proof.
options.note: Verses 9-12 give David's explicit reasoning and his chosen action.

options.text: He took the spear and water jug from beside Saul's head and quietly left the camp.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it omits David's stated theological reason for sparing Saul.

options.text: He debated killing Saul and let Abishai make the final call.
options.note: The opposite is true; David commanded Abishai not to touch Saul, the decision was David's alone.

options.text: He killed Saul immediately with Abishai's spear.
options.note: David explicitly forbade this and refused to strike Saul at all.

quiz.q: Why does David refuse to kill Saul, in his own stated words?

options.text: Because Saul is the LORD's anointed, and it is God alone who must remove him, whether by death, battle, or His own timing.
options.note: Verses 9-11 give this as David's reason, twice repeated.

options.text: Because killing a reigning king would break Israelite law.
options.note: David never appeals to a legal statute; his stated reason is Saul's anointed office, not a point of law.

options.text: Because Abishai talked him out of striking.
options.note: The reverse is true; Abishai urged the killing and David refused him.

options.text: Because he feared Saul's army would retaliate if he was caught.
options.note: The text gives no such fear; David and Abishai left completely undetected.

quiz.q: What proof did David use to confront Abner about failing to protect Saul?

options.text: The king's spear and water jug, taken from right beside Saul's sleeping head.
options.note: Verse 16 has David point to these missing items as evidence Abner failed his duty.

options.text: A report from spies that Saul's guard had grown careless.
options.note: David did send spies earlier, verse 4, but the direct proof shown to Abner was the physical spear and jug.

options.text: David's own unsupported testimony, without any physical evidence.
options.note: David had visible, physical proof in hand, not merely his word.

options.text: The corner of Saul's robe cut off earlier.
options.note: That was the proof used in chapter 24, at En-gedi, not in this later episode.

quiz.q: How does Saul respond when he recognizes David's voice and sees the evidence?

options.text: He confesses that he has sinned and played the fool, and he blesses David, predicting he will prevail.
options.note: Verses 21 and 25 record Saul's confession and his blessing over David.

options.text: He apologizes but insists he has done nothing wrong.
options.note: Saul explicitly says, 'I have sinned,' which is more than a bare apology without admission.

options.text: He orders his men to seize David at once.
options.note: Saul does the opposite, calling David 'my son' and promising never to harm him again.

options.text: He denies that the voice calling to him is David's at all.
options.note: Saul immediately recognizes and names David's voice, verse 17.

quiz.q: What does David ask the LORD to do in verse 23?

options.text: To repay every man for his righteousness and faithfulness, since David would not harm the LORD's anointed though he had the chance.
options.note: Verse 23 records this exact request, resting David's reward on God's just character.

options.text: To grant David the throne immediately, that same day.
options.note: David does trust God for his future, but his stated request here is about righteous reward, not an immediate crowning.

options.text: To punish Saul at once for hunting him.
options.note: David explicitly leaves Saul's fate to God's timing in verse 10, not to instant punishment here.

options.text: To make Saul forget the whole episode had happened.
options.note: David wants the episode remembered as proof of his innocence, not forgotten.

quiz.q: How does David's restraint in this chapter point toward Christ?

options.text: Like David refusing to seize the throne by violence, Jesus refused to grasp His kingdom by force, trusting the Father's justice and timing instead.
options.note: Matthew 26:52-53 and Philippians 2:6-11 draw exactly this connection.

options.text: It shows only that violence is generally an unwise strategy.
options.note: There is a true principle here, but it understates the specific theological point about God's anointed and God's timing.

options.text: It has no real connection to Christ, being only political strategy on David's part.
options.note: Scripture itself draws this connection through the pattern of the anointed one and the theme of entrusting oneself to God's justice.

options.text: It shows David lacked the courage that Christ later displayed.
options.note: The text presents David's restraint as deliberate conviction and courage, not cowardice.

questions: David again had Saul's life in his hands but chose to leave vindication to God. Where are you tempted to 'help God along' by taking justice into your own hands rather than trusting His timing?
David calls Saul 'the LORD's anointed' even while fleeing his violence. How does honoring the office or position someone holds change the way you deal with people who wrong you?
Saul confesses his sin, yet the pattern of hostility repeats, and David still keeps his distance. How do you discern between someone's genuine sorrow and a proven change in behavior?
David asks the LORD, not Saul, to repay his righteousness. Where do you look for validation or reward instead of resting in God's approval?

Jesus refused to seize His kingdom by force, entrusting Himself to the Father's justice. What would it look like for you to release a difficult situation into God's hands this week rather than forcing an outcome?

translationNotes.note: The LORD's anointed renders mashiach, the same word later used of the Messiah; here it names Saul's God-given office rather than his personal character, which is why David honors it even while fleeing Saul's violence.

Either his day will come and he will die, or he will go into battle and perish leaves the manner and timing of Saul's end entirely open, an intentional refusal by David to specify how or when, since that belongs to God alone.

If the LORD has stirred you up... may He accept an offering, but if men have done it, may they be cursed reflects an ancient assumption that unexplained royal hostility might have a hidden divine cause, while still holding any human instigators fully responsible for their part.

meta.notes: BSB text for 1 Samuel 26:1-25 fetched this session from bible.helloao.org. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none appear anywhere in this file.
